A film only Nolan could have made the way he did. His non-linear mastery turns three timelines into a relentless, immersive experience that rewards repeat viewings while remaining overwhelming on a sensory level.

This is one of the movies I would most like to see if it ever came back around to theaters (unfortunately I was only 8 when it was originally released). Such stunning cinematography. The wide shots of the planes flying over the water are breathtaking. What a beautifully made, realistic, moving film. I highly recommend this movie - it is appropriate even for young teens who are not too sensitive to peril (there is quite surprisingly minimal violence in this movie, especially for a war movie) - but there are several scenes where various characters are drowning or almost drown, and they are stressful and kind of hard to watch. There are only two f words in this movie which are both quick and not in-your-face, and that is the only swearing. I am very pleased with the level of violence and swearing in this movie, especially violence, because that is the main thing that bothers me usually in movies; I am 16 and very sensitive to violence, but this movie did not disturb me at all. It was just such a cool and incredible film. The end sequence is so moving. The soundtrack is a masterpiece. Hans Zimmer and Chris Nolan together are undefeatable. The cast is also amazing. Fionn Whitehead really impressed me, especially for being less well-known and also quite young. I had never seen Harry Styles in a film, but he is one of my favorite music artists, and I was pleasantly surprised at his performance in the movie. I must confess the main reason I watched the movie was because Harry Styles and Cillian Murphy are in it. But let me tell you I am very glad I saw it, not just because of the scenery. 😂 And Tom Hardy's storyline is simply so amazing, I love his character so very much. Anyhow, getting back to the point... This movie is a 10/10, I have watched it 3 times in the past 6 months and never get tired of it. Go watch it. :)

Just watched this remarkable film again at the cinema. Nolan's extraordinary writing and directing captured so much of what I can only imagine, having no semblance of a personal connection or experience w/ the events depicted or battle of any sort, this stretch of time during the war was like on the beach at Dunkirk as well as the air & sea. It's riveting stuff from the opening scene, with only brief respites between the perpetual struggle to survive and defeat the enemies. The visual work of cinematographer van Hoytema and the editing of Lee Smith must be greatly commended as well for giving us the recreation of place & time and stitching together what to keep & what to cut to make it work so incredibly well. The same goes for the sound crew and composition score by Zimmer. Crowley's production design, the visual effects, and set design/artdirection is all stellar. Nolan works with the best, and this is the standard of art they produce. 4.2 stars
Best cinematic experience in a long time. The score, sound effects, and shooting were a masterpiece

Greetings from Lithuania. First of all - "Dunkirk" (2017) is not the best war film ever made in my opinion. By far. I don't think that there ever will be a better war movies then "Apocalypse Now" (1979) or even "Saving Private Ryan" (1998), "Platoon" (1986) or "The Thin Red Line" (1998). It is not even the best Christopher Nolan movie at the moment (the crown goes to "Interstellar (2014)). That said, "Dunkirk" is something absolutely unique, stunning, kinda mesmerizing movie going experience. It is not perfect, sometimes even a bit confusing (because of the time lines, but its not "overdone") movie, but the one which absolutely has to be seen in a movie theater. The best thing about "Dunkirk" is the craftsmanship and the biggest star of "Dunkirk" is without a doubt Christopher Nolan. The script in here is not a great one, and like i said i was a bit confused at some moments, but the storytelling here is so powerful itself that it tells everything. Music is simply astonishing. Hans Zimmer (again) does something absolutely unique with Nolan movie - it transcendence the whole experience. Cinematography by Hoyte Van Hoytema is a masterpiece. Sound design and sound mixing are so good, so sharp that it adds more to this movie then just a sound - it makes you kinda grab your seat. So here are the 4 Oscar nominations for sure - best director, music, cinematography (probably a winner) and sounds (sure winners). Acting was good given the roles, but nothing very memorable except for a great Mark Rylance and Fionn Whitehead which i really liked in this role. Overall, "Dunkirk" is not a bloody or gore war movie. Its "horror" is terror. The situation itself and how it is presented in this movie made me think couple of times about the war that this is a true terror. This is not an "easy" movie going experience because you have to pay attention during a whole movie and it doesn't have sharp characters to which you can relate - its about a situation at the moment - right here right now. There are "time line" sections / intersections that makes you think about what you are seeing. And it is done so unique, so ambitious that i probably haven't witnessed this kinda movie before. "Dunkirk" is not a mainstream "war movie", basically it is a thriller set in a WW2. But its crafted so uniquely that it can not be missed. A sure classic, if not for everyone.

Christopher Nolan (director) delivers another masterpiece with Dunkirk, a war film that immerses viewers in the chaos and tension of World War II. The story focuses on the 1940 evacuation of Allied soldiers from Dunkirk, France, when German forces trapped hundreds of thousands on the beaches. The film’s structure is unique, following three overlapping timelines: land, sea, and air. On land, we follow Tommy (Fionn Whitehead), Peter (Tom Glynn-Carney), and Alex (Harry Styles), young soldiers desperately trying to escape. Their fear, uncertainty, and camaraderie drive the emotional core of the story, making the viewer feel every moment of danger. At sea, Mr. Dawson (Mark Rylance) and his son Peter (also Tom Glynn-Carney) sail a civilian boat alongside George (Barry Keoghan) to help rescue stranded troops. These scenes highlight courage and humanity, showing ordinary people performing extraordinary acts. In the air, Farrier (Tom Hardy) and Collins (Jack Lowden) provide air cover for the evacuation. The dogfights are intense, well-choreographed, and thrilling, giving a sense of the scale and danger of aerial warfare. The performances across the cast are excellent. Fionn Whitehead delivers a grounded, emotional portrayal of a soldier facing impossible odds. Mark Rylance and Barry Keoghan bring warmth and authenticity to the civilian storyline, while Tom Hardy’s stoic presence adds tension and heroism in the air. Nolan’s direction is meticulous, with a strong focus on tension, timing, and atmosphere. Combined with Hans Zimmer’s relentless score and stunning cinematography, the film creates a fully immersive experience without relying heavily on dialogue. Overall, Dunkirk is a gripping, emotionally powerful war film that showcases the bravery and resilience of soldiers and civilians alike. Nolan’s attention to detail, along with standout performances from Fionn Whitehead, Tom Glynn-Carney, Harry Styles, Mark Rylance, Barry Keoghan, and Tom Hardy, make this one of the most intense and memorable war films of recent years.
Christopher Nolan does it again: visuals that are beyond impressive, great actors, and a story that is cold and not involving.

Action-oriented war movie about heroes and cowards avoids realities I've always been interested in the first World War, much more so than the second. The evil that was so prevalent in World War II had its roots in The Great War, and I find the first more palatable for this reason. I've never been attracted to True Crime documentaries or procedural dramas. I'm also not a big non-fiction reader and am more of a novelist. When I was very young, I read 'All Quiet on the Western Front' and was deeply moved. I did love, love, love Saving Private Ryan. So, with slightly lower expectations than most, I decided to buy the book 'The Great War and Modern Memory'. While some passages were skippable for me, I was shocked at how much I learned. Hundreds of thousands of people died in battle after battle, all at once. I learned that irony and double-entendres were explored and reinvented during the war. There were several innovations to war-fighting that made their debut during the war, such as tanks, airplanes, machine guns, and trenches. How Christopher Nolan's movie barely explored any of these themes yet still was an adequately well-constructed and intriguing film is remarkable. Yet he did pay homage to each of the themes. The irony of the soldiers failed escape attempts after failed escape attempts, the dogfight plane sequences, and the references to the hundreds of thousands of soldiers stranded on the beaches of Dunkirk, all worked to frame the realities of the war. However, like most engaging cinema, the film presented an action-oriented escapism and glorification of the violence during the war. The bombastic soundtrack would later work for Nolan's other war-oriented film, Oppenheimer. The action sequences were replicated from possibly The Dark Knight series. But working all together, cohesively, the film showed certain sides of the war that were awe-inspiring. The things it missed, like the horrors of the trenches, the terrible leadership of the British army, the frightening introduction of tank warfare, were in some ways made up by the allusions to how close the war was to the soldiers' homes, the rallying of the civilians, the perceivability of the Germans as the common "Enemy", and some other parts of the movie. I enjoyed the movie. I didn't love, love, love it.

I like some things in this movie. It had nice visuals and some strong sounds, as well as some recognizable characters such as Harry Styles and Tom Hardy. HOWEVER, this movie seemed to have no focus on a main character, jumping perspective (fairly evenly) between 4 or 5 places/characters. I still don't know if there was even a main character. The movie jumped around a lot, making you lose attention and even making you wait eagerly for everyone to be saved and make it back home... not so they would be safe, but so the movie would be over. I felt this way, and I remember quite clearly because I watched it just a few minutes ago. While there are a few nice scenes, like the opening scene (beautiful!), but it really did not make you feel like this problem of theirs was worth caring about. The characters you meet display nearly no sense of urgency, fear, or pity. The character that (partly) accidentally killed George on the boat did a terrible job of acting like he was crying. I could tell that it was completely fake. His purpose of covering his face wasn't to keep people from seeing him cry. No one was around to see him cry, being occupied with more important things than acting childish when someone won't give you what you want. He was covering his face to show how badly his acting was. And don't get me started on the plot or solution or any of that. It was just too scattered to even bother breaking that down for you. As if they were making it up as they went, piecing together scenes from already existing, better movies, to make it more appealing or to make it seem like they weren't just making a movie for the sake of making it. Thank you for this movie, Christopher Nolan etc., but I hope I won't have to watch it all again.
Dunkirk is cinematic mastery—minimal dialogue, intense score, and real-time tension create pure immersion. Zimmer’s music and Nolan’s structure turn history into breathtaking, emotional art.

Dunkirk feels like the perfect vehicle for Christopher Nolan, a film that relies on visuals telling the story as opposed to characters blathering exposition to explain to the audience what’s going on, a trend that’s popped up fairly consistently since Inception. Never a deft hand at character development, Nolan wisely avoids focusing on the individual characters, instead choosing to examine the mass and desperate evacuation of the beach at Dunkirk via imagery. Hoyte van Hoytema’s beautiful cinematography is complimented by some great sound effects, tight editing, and a story that focuses as much on the small stories of the evacuation as the spectacle of it all. Looking at it objectively, this may be one of Nolan’s best films.

WARNING - Spoilers: I thought Nolan's film was appallingly bad. I was very keen to see it when the trailers came out, and my disappointment in the final product was crushing. I watched it twice as I'd promised to go see it with a friend and frankly, thirty minutes into my second viewing I was utterly bored. Weirdly empty beaches that should have been full of waiting Tommys, the strangely intact town with none of the heavy and wide-spread damage that Dunkirk sustained early on, silly sequences that made no sense or were patently unbelievable and absurd, like the out-of-fuel Spitfire shooting down a German bomber (more than unlikely - impossible), the torpedoed destroyer, sitting at anchor, all lit up at night, with empty decks, doing nothing at all, no sense of urgency to get the hell out of there. But then, where were the unceasing air attacks by the Luftwaffe? Why were there women in the destroyer? And how did it sink when, although the mess was completely flooded, the soldier found himself well above water on the forecastle when he exited? And that utterly ludicrous scene in the fishing boat when the British want to murder the French soldier - due to his weight? The boat was so full of water by that time that it couldn't have floated off the beach anyway, unlike the scene we got a few moments later. And what was the point? Like the utterly useless scene where the British officer accidently kills the boy in the yacht? How exactly did that contribute to the story of Dunkirk? It wasn't based on any real event. And Kenneth Branagh yelling "push that boat off" as the hospital ship sinks after being bombed. For crying out loud, he plays a FULL commander in the Royal Navy! First, he would never call a ship a "boat" - only a landsman would make that kind of mistake, and even more to the point, he would realise that there was no way in heaven that an 850 ton ship could be "pushed away" manually by the soldiers. Stupidity reaching the level of being moronic. I won't even start with the postwar Dutch minesweepers pretending to be British destroyers, or the obvious 1950s French destroyer also standing in as an RN one. I thought the days of slapping a swastika on a Sherman tank and calling it a Panzer had died with the 1970s? Nolan's refusal to use ANY CGI doomed this film from the start, as the end result demonstrates. HMCS HAIDA, preserved in Hamilton, Ontario, would have made a good stand-in for HMSs JAGUAR and JACKEL that were at Dunkirk, both of which were very similar in design and from the same time. The sad thing is that he didn't even consider using use ORP BLYSKAWIKA - not only is she a period Polish WWII destroyer painted in WWII camouflage today as a museum ship, BUT SHE WAS ACTUALLY AT DUNKIRK IN 1940! Nolan's poor decisions and paper-thin study of this important historical event can only satisfy those with only the shallowest of understanding or knowledge of this critical operation in WWII history. Frankly, the eight minutes of this event portrayed in "Atonement" leave Nolan's entire film completely in the shadows.

Um ótimo filme de guerra, que transmite com maestria as emoções vividas no momentos histórico que retrata. A "pior parte" é o filme ser muito curto, e apesar de isso fazer algumas cenas parecerem pouco exploradas também contribui para que o espectador se sinta na própria evacuação. Mesmo assim, acho que a edição, por muito boa que seja em alguns momentos, noutros acaba por cortar cenas que não deveria. (avaliação: 8.5/10)
This is one of Christopher Nolan's best. Dunkirk has amazing visuals and cinematography that makes it a must see on the big screen. And it's such an impactful film.
